title: King Of The Hill

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Opening sequence implies Homer gets hurt in some way
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation of pain/frustration
jokes.explanation: Homer's iconic catchphrase - perfectly character-integrated and quotable, but routine at this point in the series

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: They won't stop me from delivering these UNICEF pennies. Go, pennies. Help the puny children who need you.
jokes.setup: Action hero under attack with important mission
jokes.punchline: The 'important mission' is delivering pennies, said with complete seriousness
jokes.explanation: Strong absurdist humor - the contrast between McBain's heroic tone and the trivial mission is excellent

dialogue.speaker: McBain
dialogue.line: I must deliver these pennies. The fate of the nation depends on it.

jokes.text: Wow. McBain is really buffed up. You could grate cheese on those abs.
jokes.setup: Commenting on action hero's physique
jokes.punchline: Absurd but vivid comparison to a kitchen utensil
jokes.explanation: Solid visual humor with good economy - the cheese grater comparison is specific and funny

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa! Check out the guns on that guy! He's like a spatula with a head!

jokes.text: That's what I call breakneck speed. [Laughs]
jokes.setup: McBain just broke someone's neck
jokes.punchline: Pun on 'breakneck speed' after literal neck breaking
jokes.explanation: Decent dark pun with good timing, though puns after violence are somewhat routine for McBain

dialogue.speaker: McBain
dialogue.line: Now that's what I call breakneck speed!

jokes.text: Mom, a man just died. [Groans] Anyway, it's time for the church picnic.
jokes.setup: Lisa points out someone just died on TV
jokes.punchline: Marge dismisses death with 'anyway' and mundane transition
jokes.explanation: Great contrast between Lisa's moral concern and Marge's casual dismissal - perfect character work

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Someone just died on TV.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Anyway...

jokes.text: What? They had a picnic last week. No, they didn't. You just brought a bucket of chicken to church.
jokes.setup: Homer complains about another church picnic
jokes.punchline: Marge reveals Homer's 'picnic' was just bringing KFC to church
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy - Homer's lazy interpretation of 'church picnic' is perfectly Homer

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Another church picnic? Marge, why do we have to go to these things?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you love church picnics. You brought the KFC last time.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That wasn't a picnic, Marge. That was just me bringing KFC to church.

jokes.text: If God didn't want us to eat in church... he would have made gluttony a sin.
jokes.setup: Homer defending eating in church
jokes.punchline: Claims gluttony isn't a sin when it's literally one of the seven deadly sins
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - the irony of claiming gluttony isn't a sin is perfectly structured and character-driven

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Gluttony isn't a sin when you're eating the Lord's food in the Lord's house!

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Homer, gluttony is literally one of the seven deadly sins.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, nobody's perfect.

jokes.text: Ah, the joke's on you. We borrowed that jar from your table. So you just shot your own mustard.
jokes.setup: Mustard shooting contest at picnic
jokes.punchline: Ned reveals they borrowed Homer's mustard, so Homer shot his own
jokes.explanation: Decent misdirection joke with good structure, though not particularly memorable

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, we borrowed Homer's mustard for the contest.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: WHAT?! My mustard?!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Yes, and you just shot it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Nice to see you, Homer. Yeah. Out of my way, whoever you are. You're blocking the food.
jokes.setup: Polite greeting from fellow churchgoer
jokes.punchline: Homer rudely dismisses them as 'whoever you are' and only cares about food access
jokes.explanation: Pure Homer characterization - his food obsession overriding basic politeness is very on-brand

dialogue.speaker: Churchgoer
dialogue.line: Hello, Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, whatever. Who are you? Look, is there any food at this thing or not?

jokes.text: How's my kite doing, Smithers? Oh, it's, uh, soaring majestically, sir.
jokes.setup: Burns asks about his kite performance
jokes.punchline: Smithers lies about kite that's presumably doing poorly
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns/Smithers dynamic - Smithers protecting Burns from reality in a minor situation

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, how is my kite doing?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Splendidly, sir! It's... it's very high up. Very, very high. I can barely see it anymore.

jokes.text: You're it. We'll see about that. After him, Smithers. Aaah! You're not it!
jokes.setup: Children's game of tag at church picnic
jokes.punchline: Burns treats tag like serious business, commanding Smithers to chase children
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist moment - Burns applying his business ruthlessness to children's tag is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, I'm it. Chase those blasted children with extreme prejudice.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Yes, sir, Mr. Burns. Right away, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: And Smithers, don't let them reach the oak tree. That's a safe zone I refuse to recognize.

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: Help! He's actually chasing us!

jokes.text: Rod, ladies pick first. Okay. I choose... Todd.
jokes.setup: Picking teams for capture the flag, ladies pick first
jokes.punchline: Rod immediately picks his brother Todd despite being on the 'ladies' team
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from Rod's innocent interpretation of team selection rules

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Okay girls, let's pick our team. Rod, you're on the ladies' team.

dialogue.speaker: Rod
dialogue.line: Oh boy! I pick my brother Todd!

jokes.text: Oh! Oh! Oh! Bart! Bart! Bart! Over here! Over here! Um, I'll take- Rick me! Rick me! Nelson. Saving the best for last, huh, Bart? Yeah. That must be it.
jokes.setup: Milhouse desperately wants to be picked by Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart picks Nelson instead, sarcastically agreeing he's 'saving the best for last'
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy with Milhouse's desperation and Bart's casual cruelty to his best friend

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Pick me, Bart! Pick me!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm saving the best for last.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Nelson!

jokes.text: We- Sports on a Sunday? Hmm. I better check with Reverend- [Lovejoy] Oh, just play the damn game, Ned.
jokes.setup: Ned worries about playing sports on Sunday
jokes.punchline: Reverend Lovejoy cuts him off with profanity, telling him to just play
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Lovejoy's exasperation with Ned's over-religiosity is perfectly timed and in character

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Reverend, I'm worried about playing sports on Sunday. Isn't that a sin?

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Oh for crying out loud, Ned! Just play the diddly-darn game!

jokes.text: Well, you'd be surprised. He gets pretty competitive when he's drinking. Come on, Dad. We're playing capture the flag. Sorry, Son. Daddy's down for the day. Why don't you go capture me some more potato salad.
jokes.setup: Nelson says his drunk dad gets competitive
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately proves he's too drunk to play, asks for potato salad using 'capture' terminology
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between expectation and reality, with Homer's drunk wordplay being mildly clever

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: My dad gets really competitive when he drinks.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Let's play!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... can you pass me some of that potato salad? I've got my eye on it and I'm not gonna stop until it's... captured.

jokes.text: Flanders is playing? Why didn't you say so? [Grunting]
jokes.setup: Homer was too drunk to play capture the flag
jokes.punchline: Mention of Ned instantly motivates him to compete
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer characterization - his rivalry with Ned overriding his drunken laziness is spot-on

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, Flanders is playing? I'm in!

jokes.text: [Sighs] Oh. It appears... I will have to find a new fortress of solitude.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy observing the game setup
jokes.punchline: Dramatically declares need for new 'fortress of solitude' over minor inconvenience
jokes.explanation: Classic Comic Book Guy pomposity - applying Superman references to mundane situations

dialogue.speaker: Comic Book Guy
dialogue.line: Worst. Fortress of solitude. Ever.

jokes.text: Okay, Dad. They've got our flag guarded pretty good. I agree. Let's surrender.
jokes.setup: Bart strategically assessing the game situation
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately suggests surrendering without any attempt
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment - his immediate defeatism contrasts with Bart's tactical thinking

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: We're down by 20 points with two minutes left. What do you think, Dad? Should we keep fighting or give up?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Give up? That's quitter talk, son. We should surrender immediately.

jokes.text: No. Wait. I have a plan. But I'll need your underpants. Hmm. All right. But don't lose 'em. They're my only pair.
jokes.setup: Bart has a mysterious plan requiring Homer's underwear
jokes.punchline: Homer reveals he only owns one pair of underwear
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist setup with perfect Homer detail - only having one pair of underwear is both gross and believable

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, I need to borrow your underwear for my plan.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My underwear? Why would you need my underwear?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I can't tell you, it's a secret plan.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you can't have it. I only own one pair.

jokes.text: This isn't the flag. It looks more like- Ew! [Laughs] Suckers. Go, Dad!
jokes.setup: Kids tackle someone they think has the flag
jokes.punchline: They discover they're holding Homer's underwear while Bart reveals the deception
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to the underwear setup - the kids' disgust and Bart's cleverness work well together

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: We got the flag!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah! We won!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey! Those are my underwear!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Psych!

jokes.text: Time out! Time out! Oh, I don't think he can get up. Let's egg him! Yeah!
jokes.setup: Homer collapses during the game and calls timeout
jokes.punchline: Kids immediately suggest egging the helpless adult
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with kids' casual cruelty to a defeated adult - moderately effective

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Timeout!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad's down!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: We could egg him!

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: *sucks pacifier*

jokes.text: Oh, Marge. How could you let me let myself go like this? Me? I'm not the one who puts butter in your coffee.
jokes.setup: Homer blames Marge for letting him get out of shape
jokes.punchline: Marge points out Homer's disgusting habit of putting butter in coffee
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Homer's blame-shifting and the gross but believable butter-in-coffee detail

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This is your fault, Marge! You let me get out of shape!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're the one who puts butter in your coffee every morning.

jokes.text: Okay, honey. Okay. I'll start taking in all your slacks.
jokes.setup: Homer declares he's serious about getting in shape
jokes.punchline: Marge sarcastically offers to take in his pants, implying he'll fail
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge response - supportive but realistic about Homer's track record with resolutions

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'll take in your pants for you, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, that's sweet, Marge!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: No, I mean so I can let them back out when you give up.

jokes.text: I want you to keep this exercise thing under your hat. That way, I won't be embarrassed if I fail. [Barks] Oh, great. Now the cat knows.
jokes.setup: Homer asks dog to keep his exercise secret
jokes.punchline: Worried that the cat somehow learned his secret from dog's bark
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor - Homer's paranoia about pets knowing his secrets is perfectly illogical

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Santa's Little Helper, you can't tell anyone about my exercise routine. It's our secret, okay?

dialogue.speaker: Santa's Little Helper
dialogue.line: *barks*

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh no... what if Snowball somehow understood that bark and tells everyone?

jokes.text: Look, Son. Daddy got in shape. [Exhales Deeply] [Groans] Oh. I've gotta keep trying. Or I could just move that picture. No. The picture looked good where it was.
jokes.setup: Homer tries to suck in his gut to look fit
jokes.punchline: Considers moving the picture instead of actually getting fit, then decides against it
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer lazy logic - considering changing the environment instead of himself, then abandoning that too

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Maybe if I just suck in my gut... there, nice and trim!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, I could just move this picture over a few inches...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nah, too much work.

jokes.text: So what will it be, Mr. Simpson? Your usual bucket of ice cream covered with miniature pies?
jokes.setup: Apu asks Homer what he wants from the store
jokes.punchline: Homer's 'usual' is an absurdly unhealthy ice cream and pie combination
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist food combination that's both disgusting and perfectly Homer - 'miniature pies' as toppings is inspired

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: What can I get for you today, Homer?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The usual, Apu. A gallon of ice cream, a bucket of lard, and a deep-fried pie.

jokes.text: Oh, well, we do have some low-salt candy bars... and some reduced-fat soda. And our beef jerky is now nearly rectum-free.
jokes.setup: Apu lists healthy alternatives for Homer
jokes.punchline: Escalates from mild health claims to grotesque rectum content revelation
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from mild health improvements to disgusting meat revelation - 'nearly rectum-free' is a perfect gross-out line

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Homer, instead of donuts, try our new bran muffins! They're high in fiber and low in fat.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bran muffins? What's the point of living?

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: They also improve your digestion and promote regularity.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Regularity? I'm already regular. I'm regular as clockwork.

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Yes, and with our muffins, your bowel movements will be even more efficient and healthy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bowel movements? Ew!

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: In fact, Homer, the fiber content is so high that it will completely rejuvenate your colon and rectum, giving you the intestinal tract of a man half your age.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My rectum?!

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Yes, your rectum will be absolutely pristine. Smooth as a baby's bottom. We're talking world-class sphincter control here.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay, that's it. I'm leaving.

jokes.text: Oh, yes. That is a new item. No bar contains more applesauce.
jokes.setup: Homer asks about Powersauce bars
jokes.punchline: Technically true but meaningless marketing claim about applesauce content
jokes.explanation: Good satire of misleading health food marketing - technically true but completely meaningless claim

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Apu, what's in these Powersauce bars?

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: They're 100% apple!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, 100% apple? That's great!

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Well, 100% apple sauce. The rest is apple core, apple seeds, and apple stem.

jokes.text: [Mispronouncing] 'Gym'? What's a 'gym'? [Mispronounces] Oh. A 'gym.'
jokes.setup: Homer reads the word 'gym' on his Powersauce bar
jokes.punchline: Pronounces it wrong, then 'corrects' himself but still says it wrong
jokes.explanation: Iconic Homer moment - his mispronunciation of 'gym' perfectly captures his ignorance about fitness in minimal words

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Gym? Gee-why-em? Oh, you mean Jim!

jokes.text: Rainier Wolfcastle! Oh, I love your movies... and your Powersauce bars and your taught, rippling- Hey, hey, hey. That's enough.
jokes.setup: Homer meets his action hero idol at the gym
jokes.punchline: Gets uncomfortably specific about admiring Rainier's physique before being cut off
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor with Homer's inappropriate fan worship being shut down

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh my god, it's you! Rainier Wolfcastle! I've admired you for years. Your muscles, they're just so... perfectly defined. The way your pecs ripple when you walk, and your arms, those massive biceps...

dialogue.speaker: Rainier Wolfcastle
dialogue.line: Uh, thanks buddy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And your thighs! So sculpted and tan. I bet you can feel every individual muscle fiber when you—

dialogue.speaker: Rainier Wolfcastle
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I'm gonna go now.

jokes.text: Now step over to the Abdominator... and I will shout slogans at you.
jokes.setup: Rainier offers to help Homer with exercise
jokes.punchline: His method is shouting slogans, not actual instruction
jokes.explanation: Good satire of action star mentality - treating motivational slogans as actual training

dialogue.speaker: Rainier Wolfcastle
dialogue.line: I'll help you get in shape, Homer! Now drop and give me twenty!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Okay, here goes... one... two... three...

dialogue.speaker: Rainier Wolfcastle
dialogue.line: YOU CAN DO IT! FEEL THE BURN! NO PAIN, NO GAIN! YOU'RE A CHAMPION!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: I'm only on three.

dialogue.speaker: Rainier Wolfcastle
dialogue.line: THAT'S THE SPIRIT! PUSH THROUGH! YOU'RE UNSTOPPABLE!

jokes.text: Push. Harder. Go past the max. Reach over the top. Master your ass.
jokes.setup: Rainier shouting motivational slogans at Homer
jokes.punchline: Escalates from generic fitness language to crude command
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation of gym talk clichés ending in crude language - moderately effective

dialogue.speaker: Rainier
dialogue.line: Feel the burn! Push yourself! You can do it!

dialogue.speaker: Rainier
dialogue.line: Come on, Homer! Give me twenty more!

dialogue.speaker: Rainier
dialogue.line: That's it! Harder! Faster! Don't stop!

dialogue.speaker: Rainier
dialogue.line: Yeah! Now drop and give me fifty!

dialogue.speaker: Rainier
dialogue.line: Suck it in! Tighten that gut!

jokes.text: Wow, Wolfie. Two months ago I didn't know what the word dumbbell meant. This place is great.
jokes.setup: Homer praising his gym progress
jokes.punchline: His achievement is learning the word 'dumbbell,' not actual fitness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - measuring progress by vocabulary rather than physical achievement

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I've been going to the gym and I'm really making progress. I learned a new word today.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dumbbell.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's a place where you go to get... dumb.

jokes.text: Hello, handsome. Nice muscles. Care for a rubdown? Oh! Well, I'm flattered but spoken for. Oh, what the heck. Give me your number.
jokes.setup: Attractive woman compliments Homer's new physique
jokes.punchline: Homer initially resists temptation, then immediately gives in
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing Homer's weak resolve - quick turnaround from faithful to interested

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: Wow, Homer, you've really gotten in shape! You look amazing!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I've been working out and eating healthy. I'm trying to take care of myself.

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: It's so attractive. You must have so much willpower.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, I do have willpower. I can resist anything. I'm a new man.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Is that a donut?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... donuts.

jokes.text: Go ahead. Try and grab some flab. Go on! No. Not there. Not there either. Over here. I've got some. No. Here. Try my foot.
jokes.setup: Homer proudly showing off his weight loss to family
jokes.punchline: Frantically directs them to find flab, revealing he hasn't lost as much as claimed
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy showing Homer's self-deception about his progress

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look at me! I've lost 50 pounds!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, let me find it. It's gotta be here somewhere.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The flab! Find the flab! It didn't just disappear!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, maybe you miscounted.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Miscounted?! I know my own flab! Check behind the couch!

jokes.text: Look at you people. Bart's a tub. Lisa's weak as a little girl. And Maggie doesn't seem to be growing at all.
jokes.setup: Homer criticizes his family's fitness
jokes.punchline: Points out Lisa is weak 'as a little girl' when she literally is a little girl
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer logic failure - criticizing Lisa for being exactly what she is, a little girl

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, you're as weak as a little girl!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, I am a little girl.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly my point.

jokes.text: You too, Marge. If you toned up a little, you'd probably get a lot more action. [Clicks Tongue]
jokes.setup: Homer continues criticizing family members
jokes.punchline: Inappropriately suggests Marge needs to get in shape for sexual reasons
jokes.explanation: Effective cringe moment with Homer's inappropriate comment, though somewhat predictable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, you need to get in shape. For me. Sexually.

jokes.text: Homer, stop insulting us and eat your filth. Food. I mean food.
jokes.setup: Marge telling Homer to stop criticizing and eat
jokes.punchline: Accidentally calls his food 'filth' then corrects herself
jokes.explanation: Good Freudian slip moment - Marge's true feelings about Homer's diet leak out

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, stop criticizing and eat your food.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It's not filth— I mean, it's not... it's delicious.

jokes.text: Sorry. I only eat food in bar form. When you concentrate food, you unleash its awesome power, I'm told.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining his new diet philosophy
jokes.punchline: Believes concentrated food has 'awesome power' based on marketing claims
jokes.explanation: Good satire of health food marketing and Homer's gullibility - 'awesome power' language is perfect

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You see, I've figured out that concentrated food has awesome power. It says so right on the label! All those other foods are just diluted with water and air.

jokes.text: That's why I'm compressing five pounds of spaghetti... into one handy, mouth-size bar.
jokes.setup: Homer demonstrating his food compression theory
jokes.punchline: Literally compressing spaghetti into a bar form
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist visual - taking the bar concept to logical extreme with compressed spaghetti

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: See, if you compress the spaghetti, it becomes a candy bar. So technically, I'm not eating unhealthy food.

jokes.text: [Gulps] Hmm. [Beeping] [Line Rings] Hospital, please.
jokes.setup: Homer eats his compressed spaghetti bar
jokes.punchline: Immediately needs medical attention
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the spaghetti bar setup - immediate consequences told with economy

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm, compressed spaghetti bar.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, no!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... *chokes and gasps*

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Call an ambulance!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: *wheezing* ...need... hospital...

jokes.text: Homer, please remember to wipe your sweat off that machine. I got a terrible rash yesterday.
jokes.setup: Gym patron asking Homer to clean equipment
jokes.punchline: Reveals they already got a rash from Homer's previous sweat
jokes.explanation: Decent gross-out humor showing Homer's inconsideration at the gym

dialogue.speaker: Gym patron
dialogue.line: Hey Homer, can you clean off that equipment? You left a lot of sweat on it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure, no problem!

dialogue.speaker: Gym patron
dialogue.line: Actually, never mind. I already got a rash from your previous sweat.

jokes.text: Yeah. He helped me blast through the burn and ride the zone.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining Rainier's help
jokes.punchline: Uses meaningless fitness jargon he clearly doesn't understand
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier fitness slogans, showing Homer parroting language he doesn't understand

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You see, Rainier helped me understand that I need to focus on my core strength and maximize my cardiovascular output while maintaining optimal metabolic synergy.

jokes.text: Picture this. You- I love it. climb the highest mountain in Springfield. The Murderhorn. Are you crazy? That's suicide.
jokes.setup: Marketing executive pitching idea to Rainier
jokes.punchline: Rainier agrees before hearing the actual dangerous proposal
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with Rainier's premature enthusiasm followed by immediate regret

dialogue.speaker: Brad
dialogue.line: Mr. Wolfcastle, I have a brilliant marketing idea for your next action film.

dialogue.speaker: Rainier Wolfcastle
dialogue.line: I'm listening.

dialogue.speaker: Brad
dialogue.line: We film the entire movie while you're actually skydiving without a parachute.

dialogue.speaker: Rainier Wolfcastle
dialogue.line: Sold! When do we start?

jokes.text: Not for all the applesauce in Glückenschlabel.
jokes.setup: Rainier refusing to climb the mountain
jokes.punchline: Uses fake German place name in applesauce-themed rejection
jokes.explanation: Clever fake German name that sounds authentic while maintaining the applesauce theme

dialogue.speaker: Rainier Wolfcastle
dialogue.line: I'm not climbing that mountain. I'm afraid of heights!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Come on, Rainier! You're a big action star!

dialogue.speaker: Rainier Wolfcastle
dialogue.line: That's just a character. In real life, I can't even look at a stepladder without thinking of applesauce und Schnitzelgruben.

jokes.text: My dad could do it. He eats 50 dollars' worth of Powersauce bars a day.
jokes.setup: Bart defending Homer's capabilities
jokes.punchline: Measures Homer's qualifications by expensive junk food consumption
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Bart's faith in Homer based on completely irrelevant criteria

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: My dad may not be the smartest guy, but he's consumed more expensive junk food than anyone I know. That's gotta count for something.

jokes.text: Overshoot the extreme. Max the envelope and so on.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to sound impressive to marketers
jokes.punchline: Trails off with 'and so on' after running out of meaningless slogans
jokes.explanation: Good satire of empty marketing language with Bart's awkward trailing off

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Buy Butterfinger! It's great! So crispy, so chocolatey, so... and so on.

jokes.text: It's believable. That's what I like about it.
jokes.setup: Discussing Homer climbing a deadly mountain
jokes.punchline: Marketing executive thinks this obviously absurd plan is 'believable'
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of marketing delusion - calling an impossible plan 'believable' is perfectly absurd

dialogue.speaker: Brad
dialogue.line: A man climbing a deadly mountain? That's not believable.

dialogue.speaker: Marketing Executive
dialogue.line: Not believable? This is gold! Americans will eat this up. It's got danger, it's got heart, it's got a guy doing something stupid. This is totally believable.

jokes.text: Uh, no. That's it over there. [Gasps] Yeah. That's it. Just to the right of the one you're looking at.
jokes.setup: Homer looks at a mountain
jokes.punchline: Keeps being directed to an even more intimidating peak
jokes.explanation: Standard escalation gag about the mountain's intimidating size

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, look at that mountain. I'm gonna climb it!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, what? You're telling me there's a bigger one behind it?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not giving up. I'll climb that one too!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh no, there's another one? This is ridiculous!

jokes.text: [Whimpers] [Homer looks at wrong mountain] [Whimpers] Oh.
jokes.setup: Homer sees the mountain he has to climb
jokes.punchline: First looks at wrong mountain, then sees the real one and whimpers
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy with Homer's fear escalating when he sees the real challenge

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Whimper

jokes.text: Sure. It was the winter of '28. The Butter Baby Flapjack Company sponsored me and a fella named McAllister.
jokes.setup: Grampa tells story about climbing the same mountain
jokes.punchline: Was sponsored by company with ridiculous name 'Butter Baby Flapjack Company'
jokes.explanation: Excellent fake company name that sounds both period-appropriate and absurd - perfect Grampa storytelling

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I climbed that mountain once. It was sponsored by the Butter Baby Flapjack Company.

jokes.text: Heroically, I gave him the last short stack.
jokes.setup: Grampa describing his heroic mountain climbing sacrifice
jokes.punchline: His 'heroic' act was giving away pancakes
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa logic - treating pancake sharing as heroic sacrifice in life-or-death situation

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I climbed that mountain and sacrificed everything. I gave away my pancakes to the other climbers.

jokes.text: Of course, folks were tougher in those days. I was jitterbugging that very night.
jokes.setup: Grampa fell 8,000 feet onto jagged rocks
jokes.punchline: Claims he was dancing later that same night
jokes.explanation: Excellent Grampa exaggeration - the contrast between deadly fall and immediate dancing is perfectly absurd

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I fell 8,000 feet onto jagged rocks.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad, that's terrible! You must be seriously injured.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Injured? Pfft! I was dancing later that same night!

jokes.text: Hey. My hat has earflaps.
jokes.setup: Grampa trying to discourage Homer from climbing
jokes.punchline: Homer gets distracted by trivial hat feature instead of focusing on danger
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer attention span - completely missing the point about danger to focus on hat features

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Don't climb up there, boy! You'll fall and break your neck!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Don't worry, Dad. I've got this... Hey, is this hat brim reflective?

jokes.text: Wait. Is 'contempt' the word I want here?
jokes.setup: Marketing executive describing Homer's mission as showing 'contempt for nature'
jokes.punchline: Realizes mid-speech this might not be the best marketing message
jokes.explanation: Good meta-moment about corporate messaging accidentally revealing true attitudes

dialogue.speaker: Brad
dialogue.line: We're showing contempt for nature, which is perfect because— wait, no, that's terrible. We can't say that.

jokes.text: That's right, Brad and Neil. I only eat Powersauce, the bar with 'applesauceicity.'
jokes.setup: Homer giving interview about his diet
jokes.punchline: Makes up nonsense word 'applesauceicity' to describe the bars
jokes.explanation: Great invented word that sounds like corporate marketing speak - 'applesauceicity' is perfectly meaningless

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you see, these bars have a very high applesauceicity.

jokes.text: Don't touch my stuff. Dad, wait. You're not risking your life just to impress me, are you? Well, yeah. Oh, cool. Now get going. Chop-chop.
jokes.setup: Bart asks if Homer is risking his life to impress him
jokes.punchline: When Homer confirms, Bart casually tells him to hurry up and risk death
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with Bart's casual acceptance of Homer's potential death for his entertainment

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you're not risking your life just to impress me, are you?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yes, son, I am.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, hurry up and risk death already.

jokes.text: Oh, man. This is hard. Uh, Dad... shouldn't you save a couple bottles for when you're a little higher?
jokes.setup: Homer struggling early in the climb
jokes.punchline: Already using oxygen at the very beginning of the mountain
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy showing Homer's complete unpreparedness for the challenge

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why do I need oxygen already? We just started climbing!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, we're only at the base of the mountain.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, but I'm already exhausted. Maybe I should've skipped the donuts this morning.

jokes.text: Don't get discouraged, Dad. Only four vertical miles to go.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to encourage Homer
jokes.punchline: 'Encouragement' emphasizes the enormous remaining distance
jokes.explanation: Good ironic encouragement that actually highlights how impossible the task is

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Come on, Dad! You can do it! You're almost there!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Almost where, boy?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, that's the thing about 'encouragement' — it really emphasizes just how enormous the remaining distance is.

jokes.text: Wow. Look how far I climbed. And I'm not even tired.
jokes.setup: Homer wakes up and assesses his progress
jokes.punchline: Claims he's not tired (after sleeping) and climbed far (while being dragged)
jokes.explanation: Good visual irony - Homer taking credit for progress made while unconscious

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not tired, and I've climbed so far!

jokes.text: Brad, could Homer have made it this far... without the engineered nutrition of six kinds of apples? No way, Neil. Oh. This just in. Powersauce is amazing!
jokes.setup: News anchors discussing Homer's progress
jokes.punchline: Blatantly fake 'breaking news' that's just a Powersauce ad
jokes.explanation: Good satire of sponsored content disguised as news reporting

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We interrupt this program for breaking news.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Homer Simpson has made remarkable progress in his fitness journey, thanks to Powersauce bars!

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Powersauce: now with 15% more yellow.

jokes.text: Brad and Neil were quite insistent you not die. Frankly, we don't care.
jokes.setup: Homer discovers Sherpas have been helping him
jokes.punchline: Sherpa bluntly states they don't care if Homer dies
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan delivery of dark truth - the Sherpa's honesty about not caring is brutally funny

dialogue.speaker: Sherpa
dialogue.line: We don't care if you die.

jokes.text: Then technically shouldn't you go back down and start all over? Shut up! You are so fired!
jokes.setup: Homer claims he'll climb alone, Sherpa points out logical flaw
jokes.punchline: Homer fires them for being logically correct
jokes.explanation: Good logic joke with Homer's anger at being called out on obvious contradiction

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't need any of you! I'm climbing this mountain alone!

dialogue.speaker: Sherpa
dialogue.line: But sir, if you climb alone, who will carry your supplies and guide you safely?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're fired! All of you! I don't pay you to make sense!

jokes.text: Wake up, Homer. Those bars are just junk. They're made of apple cores and Chinese newspapers. Hey. Deng Xiaoping died.
jokes.setup: Neil reveals Powersauce bars are made of garbage
jokes.punchline: Homer focuses on news from the Chinese newspaper rather than the health danger
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - Homer misses the point about health danger to comment on random historical fact

dialogue.speaker: Neil
dialogue.line: The Powersauce bar contains 100% of the recommended daily allowance of fiber, which comes from the recycled newspaper printed with soy-based inks.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. Did you say Chinese newspaper?

jokes.text: Homer Simpson, defying all medical advice... has switched to Powersauce's archrival, the Vita-Peach Health Log.
jokes.setup: News continues covering Homer's climb
jokes.punchline: Reports he switched to rival product with equally ridiculous name
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist product name and media satire escalation

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We continue our live coverage of Homer Simpson's mountain climb, where he has just switched to a rival energy drink with an equally ridiculous name.

jokes.text: Doctors say he may not have the mega-nutrients needed to stave off death.
jokes.setup: News report about Homer switching to different energy bars
jokes.punchline: Uses ridiculous term 'mega-nutrients' for basic nutrition
jokes.explanation: Decent satire of health food marketing language but not particularly memorable

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: This just in: Local man Homer Simpson has switched to a new energy bar, claiming it contains mega-nutrients.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: When asked what mega-nutrients are, Mr. Simpson simply said 'I dunno, but they sound mega.'

jokes.text: Oh, Dad's gonna die, and it's all my fault. I really need a good talking-to.
jokes.setup: Bart realizes his father might die because of him
jokes.punchline: Understates it as needing 'a good talking-to'
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Bart's childish understanding of appropriate consequences

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad might die because of me... I guess he just needs a good talking-to.

jokes.text: Air's getting thin. But I'm gonna make it. Ooh. Getting light-headed. Good. No heavy head to carry.
jokes.setup: Homer experiencing altitude sickness symptoms
jokes.punchline: Misinterprets 'light-headed' literally as having less weight to carry
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer logic - taking 'light-headed' literally and finding a positive spin is perfect character work

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Light-headed? I feel great! All this altitude stuff is making me lighter. I must weigh less now!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's not what light-headed means.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure it does! My head is light! I should leave it here and pick it up on the way down. Think of the savings on neck strain!

jokes.text: Wait. I'll just take that escalator.
jokes.setup: Homer struggling to climb the mountain
jokes.punchline: Hallucinates an escalator due to oxygen deprivation
jokes.explanation: Good hallucination gag that's perfectly Homer - looking for the easy way out even while delirious

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: An escalator! Finally!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Even when stationary, it's still a sweet ride.

jokes.text: Wait. I'll just take that escalator.
jokes.setup: Homer is struggling with the difficult climb
jokes.punchline: Hallucinates an escalator on the mountain
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag showing Homer's delirium and preference for the easy way

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: An escalator! Finally, a break!

jokes.text: I did it. I made it all the way to- Aw, crap! It just keeps going! [Sobbing] I give up.
jokes.setup: Homer celebrates reaching what he thinks is the summit
jokes.punchline: Discovers it's a false peak and completely breaks down
jokes.explanation: Good emotional roller coaster from triumph to despair - the false peak reveal is well-executed

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I did it! I reached the summit! Woo-hoo!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... there's another peak higher than this one.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No! No! Noooooo! Why?! Why would you do this to me?!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I can't go on. I just can't.

jokes.text: Worse still, that treacherous skunk, Abe Simpson... stole my oxygen and tried to eat my left arm.
jokes.setup: Homer reading McAllister's final diary entry
jokes.punchline: Reveals Grampa's version of events was completely false - he was the villain
jokes.explanation: Excellent reversal of Grampa's heroic story - revealing him as a treacherous cannibal is darkly brilliant

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let me read Principal McAllister's final diary entry...

dialogue.speaker: Principal McAllister
dialogue.line: Dear diary, today I finally understand the truth. Everything Grampa Simpson told me was a lie. He was the villain all along.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, what? Grampa's been lying this whole time?

jokes.text: Tell my beloved wife my last thoughts were of her... blinding and torturing Abe Simpson. Cheerio.
jokes.setup: McAllister's dying words about his beloved wife
jokes.punchline: His loving thoughts are about her torturing Abe
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy - subverts romantic dying words with revenge fantasy

dialogue.speaker: McAllister
dialogue.line: Tell my wife... I love her. Tell her I love her very much.

dialogue.speaker: McAllister
dialogue.line: She used to torture the old man... oh, how I loved watching that.

jokes.text: My dad's a disgrace, just like Bart's dad! Me! [Sobs]
jokes.setup: Homer lamenting that his dad is a disgrace like Bart's dad
jokes.punchline: Realizes he IS Bart's dad
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - the delayed realization that he's criticizing himself is brilliantly structured

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My dad is a disgrace, just like Bart's dad.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... I AM Bart's dad!

jokes.text: This may not be the top, but it's as close as a Simpson's ever gonna get.
jokes.setup: Homer accepts his limitations
jokes.punchline: Sets low expectations for the entire Simpson family line
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware Homer moment, accepting family mediocrity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not going to kill myself over this. I accept my limitations. After all, I'm just an average guy with an average life.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Besides, why should I set my expectations high? I'll just pass my low expectations on to Bart, he'll pass them on to his kids, and so on. That way, the whole Simpson family line will have low expectations.

jokes.text: This is for you, Son.
jokes.setup: Homer planting flag despite not being at the real summit
jokes.punchline: Dedication to Bart from false peak shows determination despite failure
jokes.explanation: Sweet moment that shows Homer's love for Bart even in his confused state

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm going to plant this flag right here at the summit!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, that's not the real summit. We're still 200 feet below.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, boy. But I'm dedicating this flag to you anyway. It shows I've got the determination to keep going... even if I fail.

jokes.text: I climbed the unclimbable mountain! Bow down before me, everyone, for I am your king!
jokes.setup: Homer discovers he actually did reach the real summit
jokes.punchline: Immediately declares himself king of everyone
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer ego inflation - immediately goes from achievement to declaring dominion over humanity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm king of the world! King of everyone!

jokes.text: Uh, I'm pretty sure he's dead, little girl. Here. Have a Powersauce bar. It's on the house.
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to contact Homer by radio
jokes.punchline: Apu casually declares Homer dead and offers free product as consolation
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with Apu's casual death pronouncement and opportunistic product placement

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Homer, Homer, do you copy?

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: He is dead. Please accept this free Squishee as my condolences.

jokes.text: Oh, no! He fell! I can't look! [Faint Shouting] Hi.
jokes.setup: Family thinks Homer fell to his death
jokes.punchline: Homer casually greets them, having survived impossible fall
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with dramatic buildup to casual Homer greeting - classic cartoon physics

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, guys!

jokes.text: Where in the world did you find a sled on top of the- [Screams]
jokes.setup: Marge questioning Homer's sled
jokes.punchline: Cut off mid-sentence by seeing something horrifying
jokes.explanation: Setup for visual gag that's probably funnier in animation than transcript

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what are you doing with that sled? It's the middle of summer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well Marge, I figured I'd get a head start on next winter and—

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that doesn't make any sense. You can't just... Oh my God!

jokes.text: It looks like someone tried to eat him. What are you looking at me for?
jokes.setup: Examining what appears to be Homer's corpse
jokes.punchline: Homer defensively asks why they're looking at him when cannibalism is mentioned
jokes.explanation: Good dark callback to the cannibalism theme with Homer's guilty defensive reaction

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: We got a real mystery here. No signs of struggle, no wounds...

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Could be cannibalism.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey! Why are you looking at me?

jokes.text: All I see is a pole. Maybe the flag fell off. Ah, damn it!
jokes.setup: Bart looking for evidence Homer reached the summit
jokes.punchline: Homer's accomplishment can't be verified due to missing flag
jokes.explanation: Decent ironic ending where Homer's genuine achievement can't be proven

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, where's the flag? Where's the picture of you at the summit?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I was gonna take one, but I didn't have a camera.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What about the flag? Climbers always plant a flag at the top.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I did plant a flag! A big one!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Then where is it?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The wind blew it away.

jokes.text: Is that your wallet? D'oh!
jokes.setup: Bart spots something that fell out of Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer's classic 'D'oh!' at losing his wallet
jokes.explanation: Classic episode ending with Homer's signature exclamation - routine but satisfying

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Dad, you dropped something.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

tmdb_overview: After Rainier Wolfcastle helps Homer get in shape, Homer accepts a challenge to climb the Murderhorn, the tallest mountain in Springfield.
insight: Just-below-elite 79 score anchored by character work across 83 distributed jokes.
